sentences.text: Mhm.
Yes.
No.
I can assume it's first name and address and date of birth.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I know it was something attached.
No.
No.
I was scared during the night because of the night and because of walking.
Because as a little boy, I still-- I was walking with my mother.
And now I know why.
My mother was pregnant with my brother, you know.
So she could not carry me, and I was walking with tiny feet, crossing the border, you know.
How long I was walking, I don't know.
When I was picked out, when we came to Brest, when, how we met the father-- I don't remember.
I don't remember this.
I'm sorry.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I think I did.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
No, no.
No, I don't.
Yes, I think I-- I was, you know.
I was just interested in something else, probably playing.
It was the-- it was the city.
It was a border city.
It was the border.
First it was-- the city was named Brzesc in Polish.
But in 1939, the Russians took it over, and the city was on the border.
It was right across the border.
It was the first city.
You know, that's-- apparently Father knew this city, and he came to this-- this particular place, you know, just across and be there.
Did I do what?
It was in '39.
It was in '39, somewhere, I think-- October, November.
And we lived, as I mentioned before, in Brest probably very short.
My brother was born in Brest, and he was born the 9th of May.
So probably we were there until probably-- maybe a year, maybe a year even.
I don't remember.
He was a waiter.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was his trade, his profession.
Nothing.
No.
Oh, boy, this a-- this is a separate chapter--
--schooling.
The first years in Russia, no, I didn't go to school.
After Brest, we went-- we lived-- we moved to Kremenets.
That's what the name was-- Kremenets, where my father was a waiter again.
I remember once I came with my mother to his place where he was working.
And my mother, since the-- since the beginning, she taught me how to dance and how to sing.
So we walked in into the restaurant where my father was a waiter.
A lot of people were there.
And all a sudden, I-- I stood right in the middle of the restaurant and started to sing.
And I started to dance a-- a dance of our navy man.
And I found out that people started to come in to me and give me money.
This was my first performance.
This I remember.
My dad, he was very upset with it, because he said, probably to my mother, you shouldn't come here.
We're busy, and I'm working.
That's how it go.
In Kremenets, my first-- my first coming to books-- because this my-- one of my few hobbies.
I loved collect books and deal with books-- is we were living next door to us was living a rabbi.
And the rabbi start to teach me the first letter of Hebrew, the alphabet.
I remember-- [SPEAKING HEBREW].
And he was sitting, and he was teaching me.
And I was very impatient, apparently.
Whenever he used to go out, I used to open his-- his-- his place where he used to keep the books, you know.
And I used to look through the books.
And some of them, I start to play around with it.
He was very upset with it, that I was browsing in his books.
But this was my first-- first-- but I did not learn anything, just few letters I learned.
Because right then-- right then, the war broke out between Russia and-- and Germany.
Germans invaded-- invaded, you know-- invaded Russia, you know.
And we moved-- moved away from there.
I did not feel it.
I did not feel it.
I personally didn't feel it.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] are you asking for anti-Semitism, something like this?
Maybe there were outbursts.
But I don't recall, and I don't recall that my mother would say or my father would say, this is a bad place to be, you know.
It's-- you know.
I don't recall this.
Kremenets?
I think it was 1940.
I was-- I was then four years old.
I don't remember.
It was the war, and war started, and we moved.
Oh, boy-- now I have a little bit-- and we moved-- moved to-- to the Caucasian Mountains.
We lived in-- in the Russian part, but closer to Caucasian Mountains.
We moved, and how we moved, I don't remember.
And over there, not only we, but I think it was 12 more Jewish families, all refugees from Poland.
They settled us in a kolkhoz, in a collective farm.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, you know-- and that's where my father and my mother started to work, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my father was working in a field, you know, in wheat or rye or whatever.
He did not have shoes, because Russia was poor.
And so I remember he used to wear rags on his feet during wintertime.
And that's how he used to go to work in the field.
My mother worked, during the summer-- my-- on the grapes.
She used to pick up grapes, taking care of grapes, you know, it was-- it was not a poor farm, you know.
It was not a poor farm.
Otherwise, obviously, they wouldn't settle us there.
And we lived in a hut.
They gave us a hut afterwards.
And I think it was October or November of 1941 when my father went volunteer-- volunteered to go to the Russian army-- volunteered.
The first time he went there, he came back, because he said they said that you have two children, apparently.
But later on, probably they drafted him.
It's another chapter-- it's beautiful chapter-- how my mother and father later found out each other in 1945, beginning.
But this is later, probably.
We'll get to it.
1941-- '41, November somewhere, October, November, my father went voluntarily to serve in the army.
No.
As a child?
No, no.
I was a child.
I remember I was in a-- in the kindergarten.
So was my-- my brother.
My brother was in-- in in a nursery.
He was a baby.
And I was in kindergarten.
And I remember how they used to feed us in this kindergarten.
It was a big, big circle of children, about 20 children.
In the middle of this circle was the teacher with a bowl of-- of food.
I don't remember what kind of food.
And she had one spoon.
And she was feeding-- she was feeding with this one spoon.
First she tried the portion on the spoon.
Then she used to give each child, the next time-- and she used to feed with one spoon all 15 or 20 children.
That's what I remember this.
It was something-- as a child, I-- I thought I had a feeling it's something weird.
It shouldn't be like this.
But that's how she used to feed-- she used to feed us.
Yes.
Nothing.
I don't remember.
I started to talk Russian, apparently.
I don't know.
Yes, it was not bad.
It was not bad.
We had food.
We had food.
We had a shelter, you know, a place where to stay and to sleep.
It was not bad.
As I mentioned, it was a-- kolkhoz are better off, kolkhoz.
It was by the Caucasian Mountains, in the South of Russia, not far from the Caspian Sea.
Now I can say it.
Between Caspian Sea and Black Sea.
He was-- he volunteered, and he came back, and then he went back.
It was a matter of, I think-- I don't-- I can't-- a matter of, I think, weeks or months, you know.
No.
No.
No.
No.
Only when he-- well, when he came-- no, I didn't see.
It-- he went-- he did not go-- he didn't go in a uniform.
They didn't give him a uniform.
He was drafted.
He even didn't know where he would be.
'41, the end of '41.
That's correct.
[SIGHS] After working-- after working a whole season, my mother received-- and this is all what I don't remember, but my mother told me.
My mother received-- instead of payment, she received a sack or two sacks of wheat, you know.
And she could sell it.
She sold the wheat.
Then the Germans apparently start to approach-- approach this region, you know, the Caucasian region.
So we left.
I remember we approached-- approached-- we were sleeping on the street of the city of Astrakhan-- the city of Astrakhan.
It's a port city of the Caspian Sea.
I remember this, the sleeping on the streets.
And we were not the only one.
There was a whole long row of-- of people, called bezhenets, means refugees, waiting for, apparently, a barge.
And I remember we boarded a barge.
A lot of people were on a barge to cross the Caspian Sea on the other side to the Asian part.
And when we crossed on the Asian part, this I remember.
I don't know how it happened, but apparently those refugees had transportation.
The transportation were on oxen.
Oxen and wagons were provided for for these refugees I don't know how.
And we were all loaded on these wagons.
And we went through, I remember, days and days-- and the heat was terrible-- through a-- through a-- oh, gosh-- through Karakum, through the-- you know, through the-- the wilderness of Karakum.
There were-- we did not have water.
We met there nomads, still nomads, Turkish descent.
They tried to help us.

About 12 or 15 days we were traveling this-- this way, until we reached-- we reached another kolkhoz, already in Asia, in Kazakhstan-- in Kazakhstan, where my mother worked in field by potatoes, collect potatoes, worked on picking out potatoes.
In this-- in this village, we did not live in a house.
We lived in a-- well, in a bunker in the earth.
It was-- I cannot say it right.
It was inside.
You had to go down to the earth a little.
The hole was covered with a little roof.
And that's where we lived with another family, with another Jewish family.
The other Jewish family was from Odessa, I recall.
And my mother used to go and work and evening used to come.

And I was staying with my brother there in this little-- little hole with the other-- I remember-- I didn't understand why, but one evening, my brother and me-- some people used to-- came and asked us to get out.
And it was a girl of the family.
There was a mother and a girl.
The girl was maybe-- I don't know-- 13, 14 years old.
And apparently she was raped there, and they were taking away to a hospital, or that's what I remember.
Then the mother and-- mother and daughter moved out.
And we were staying there in this hole, you know, in the-- and I remember one day a Kazakh approached my mother, and he wanted to buy my brother.
He was small.
He was then two years old, two and a half years old, with curly blond hair.
And he wanted to buy.
And he insisted he wanted to buy his boy.
He had to buy this boy.
Of course, my mother didn't-- didn't sell, didn't go for it-- for some wheat or something.

From there, we moved-- and I don't know what was the reasons we moved-- to a place-- used to in Russian call it stancja, which is probably a settlement-- called Chiili, also in Kazakhstan, not far from the city Kizilordu.
Over there, we had a horrible time.
I remember my mother, as a wife of a soldier-- and then we stopped knowing anything about my father.
We did not-- where he is, what's happened to him, in which parts, if he's alive or not.
We did not know anything about it.
It's even before that-- before, a few months after-- after we moved from-- from the-- from the village, from the kolkhoz.
The knowledge about him was interrupted.
And we did not want to think about it.
We came to Chiili.
We lived in a room.
It was not a room.
It was three walls.
There was three walls only.
Wintertime, we were-- wintertime we did not go out at all, because it was cold.
It was-- the fourth wall was cracked.
It was the winter, and frost was there.
And the only furniture we had was a table, similar to the-- to the-- to the picnic tables here you have.
On this table, we were sleeping, me and my brother and my mother, my mother on one side and my brother and me on the other side, the heads.
And we were covered with straw and with rags because it was cold.
My mother used to receive-- as a wife of a soldier, she was privileged to have every day 300 gram of bread for each member of family.
That was-- in other words, it's 900 gram of bread, black bread, dark, was heavy, and a bowl of soup.
So my mother used to go every day to stand in line to get this.
And she would-- the neighbors used to tell us that she was drinking up the water from the soup.
And she used to leave-- leave for us the-- the thick one, the rice or the potatoes, whatever was left, for the kids to nourish.
My mother was so weak that when it was a wind outside, she couldn't walk out.
She couldn't walk in the wind.
She had to come back.
I used to go out when my mother was inside.
I used to go out and try to find some pieces of wood, branches, to bring in to the little stove.
It was a stove made of stone.
It was warm around the stove when it was a fire there.
But when it was not fire, it was cold, terribly cold.
My mother used to warm there water.
I don't know what for.
OK, to the school, I did not go.
Then from there, my mother start to work.
I don't know-- she was working for a while.
We moved to a different place, in the same place, in the same city, I mean, the same-- Chiili.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived-- and apparently my mother picked up-- picked up a house-- apartment-- a room in the worst neighborhood.
There were thieves and robbers living there.
But-- but they didn't touch us.
Usually thieves and robbers never touch anybody from their immediate neighborhood.
So my mother used to go to work, and I used to stay with my brother all day, locked up with a little hook inside this-- this room.
There was the same furniture we had in this room.
And it was a shelf along the wall.
On the shelf was nothing.
[SIGHS] One day-- it was a summer day-- please, I was a child-- a summer day.
All of a sudden, a plane-- a two-flat plane-- I don't know how you call in this-- old fashioned plane-- landed not far from the place we used to live.
And by the way, not far-- maybe-- maybe 300 yards, a quarter of a mile-- was already the-- the wilderness, you know.
The wilderness started with sand, with dunes of sand.
And the plane landed there.
And I looked through the little window, and I see all of the kids from the neighborhood are running to see the plane.
My mother said not to go out, but I wanted to see the plane.
And so I did not pay attention what my mother said.
I took my brother on my shoulder here.
I was barefooted.
I was only in this, in the panties.
My brother was always naked walking around.
It was summertime.
It was hot.
And I took him, and I was running to see the plane, together with the kids.
But when I returned, my mother was standing in the door.
And what happened, somebody walked in into the apartment and stole this piece of bread lying on this shelf.
And I got beating from my mother.
Now I can understand why.
I got beating.
I was severely beaten.
I mean, not abused, but beating, you know.
Got beatings.
To me?
I don't remember.
I remember that she decide-- she-- I think she was-- she was a cleaning lady somewhere in an office.
I do not know.
I do not know.
I know that from this neighborhood, we moved to another place.
We were living with a shoemaker, a Jew, also from Odessa.
It was-- I think it was 1944 already.
And this-- this shoemaker had a child.
And he was making shoes, so apparently he was better off.
We were staying in the same-- he was staying in one room with his wife, apparently, and his child.
And I was staying with my mother and my brother in another room.
And I remember that the child had a piece of bread, you know, a core, you know, a piece of bread.
And she was just a child.
And she dropped it.
And I jumped to take it, and I ate it.
I was very hungry-- very hungry.
And I remember he had-- not a radio, but he had a sort of a speaker, that only what the-- what Moscow wanted us to listen, they broadcast.
And I remember the broadcast, that it was January of 1945, that Warsaw was liberated.
And then-- I don't remember if it was before this announcement or after the announcement-- that Mother came in into this room-- I never-- I don't remember before, her face was so shining.
And she screamed to me, [SPEAKING POLISH], Daddy's alive!
Because she received a letter, not from my father, but from the chairman of this kolkhoz where they used to work.
What happens, my father was injured.
He was-- he had-- maybe later I will describe.
He was-- he was under [INAUDIBLE], he was under Stalingrad, fighting.
And one day, he decide-- he decided to write a letter to the chairman of the kolkhoz where my parents used to work and with asking, maybe you know what's happened to my children and to my wife?
The chairman apparently wrote him back, but that he does not know anything about-- about your family, but I'm going to keep-- keep your letter.
Two months later, my mother wrote to this chairman, asking the same-- apparently the same question.
And the chairman sent to her my father's letter.
He wrote in Russian.
He learned-- fast learner, even though he was not an educated man.
Neither was my mother.
And he sent my mother's letter to my father, somewhere in the army, somewhere in the front.
And this was the first time they found each other.
No.
No.
Yes, never went to school.
I do remember, though, that my mother start to receive from the American organization of UNRRA-- I remember-- not I remember, but it was UNRRA that everybody was talking-- food, and packaged food.
Somewhere in '45, end of '44.
Apparently there was an agreement between-- between America-- United States-- and Russia.
They were helping.
My father got also help from-- during the-- during the-- when he was on the front.
But that's a different chapter.
Also some help, you know-- that's what-- we received the first food in package.
Package was UNRRA food.
From this place we moved to the same place.
I mean the same city-- I'm sorry-- but on a different street.
On a different street.
On a different street.
I remember the address of this street-- Ulitsa Stalina, Sorok Odin.
It means Stalin Street, number 41.
We moved to a 70-year-old widow.
She was a illiterate, but she let us in.
She rented us the room.
She had-- her-- her daughter-in-law was a teacher in the school, not from-- in this.
And at this time when my-- when we moved to this old lady, my mother decide to take care of our life in her own hands.
Namely, she start to go into business, sort of.
She went once to the market.
That's how she started.
And she bought a kilo or two or three kilos, I mean, four of five pounds of little fish-- little fish that-- you know.
She brought it home.
She grinded the fish, the little fish, made little patties.
And she went to the market with those patties, and she sold it.
She made the first profit.
Then when it came wintertime, she bought a big wrought-iron kettle, named [INAUDIBLE], I remember.
And she, especially during wintertime, she started to cook.
And when she start-- from this money, she was able to buy some food and to cook, not only for us, but also to sell in-- to sell as portions on this market.
And that's where my mother start to-- start to-- we start to live like humans.
OK.
